title: Integrate & differentiate power series
description: Practice integrating and differentiating power series, or analyzing the integrals/derivatives of power series.

hints:
Now recognize that the result is a geometric series with first term $\dfrac{1}{4}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{4}$.  Hence, the series converges to 

$\displaystyle\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4}}{1-\dfrac{1}{4}}=\dfrac{1}{4-1}=\dfrac{1}{3}$.

Now recognize that the result is a geometric series with first term $\dfrac{1}{9}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{3}$.

Hence, the series converges to

$\displaystyle\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{9}}{1-\dfrac{1}{3}}=\dfrac{1}{9-3}=\dfrac{1}{6}$

Now recognize that the result is a geometric series with first term $1$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

Hence, the series converges to

$\displaystyle\dfrac{1}{1-\dfrac{1}{2}}=2$.

Now recognize that the result is a geometric series with first term $1$ and common ratio $-\dfrac{1}{5}$.

Hence, the series converges to

$\displaystyle\dfrac{1}{1+\dfrac{1}{5}}=\dfrac{5}{6}$.
Note that because we are interested in what happens at $x=0$, we only need to pay attention to the constant term in the series that represents $f'''$.

With that said, we now calculate derivatives.  For clarity, we will work out one more term than we actually need to do the problem.

$\begin{align}
f'(x)&=3x^2-\dfrac{5{{x}^{4}}}{3!}+...
\\\\
f''(x)&=6x-\dfrac{20{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+...
\\\\
f'''(x)&=6-\dfrac{60x^2}{3!}+...
\end{align}$


So $f'''(0)=6$.
Note that because we are interested in what happens at $x=0$, we only to pay attention to the constant term in the series that represents $f'''$.

With that said, we now calculate derivatives.  For clarity, we will calculate out one more term than we actually need to do the problem.

$\begin{align}
f'(x)&=1-\dfrac{3{{x}^{2}}}{2!}+\dfrac{5{{x}^{4}}}{4!}-…
\\\\
f''x)&=-\dfrac{3\cdot 2x}{2!}+\dfrac{5\cdot 4{{x}^{3}}}{4!}-...
\\\\
f'''(x)&=-\dfrac{3\cdot 2}{2!}+\dfrac{5\cdot 4\cdot 3{{x}^{2}}}{4!}-...
\end{align}$

So $f'''(0)=-3$.
Note that because we are interested in what happens at $x=0$, we only need to pay attention to the constant term in the series that represents $f$.

With that said, we now calculate derivatives.  For clarity, we will work out one more term than we actually need to do the problem.

$\begin{align}
f'\left( x \right)&=-2+\dfrac{6{{x}^{2}}}{3!}-\dfrac{10x^4}{5!}+...
\\\\
f''\left( x \right)&=\dfrac{12x}{3!}-\dfrac{40x^3}{5!}+...
\\\\
f'''\left( x \right)&=\dfrac{12{{}^{}}}{3!}-\dfrac{120x^2}{5!}+...
\end{align}$


So $f'''\left( 0 \right)=\dfrac{12}{3!}=2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle f(x)=\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\dfrac{{{x}^{2n+3}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}}$

**Find the power series of $f''(x)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

$\displaystyle f(x)=\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\dfrac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}}$

**Find the power series of $f''(x)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
